does: SafeDIG introduces a safety steering framework for text-to-image Diffusion Transformers that addresses the progressive binding of harmful semantics through the generation process. The approach constructs Sparse Autoencoders at functionally distinct intervention positions within DiT, then applies robustness-aware pre-training routing to identify and prioritize intervention sites that remain stable when safety risks shift domains. By freezing SAE encoders as reusable sparse safety dictionaries and separating transferable safety features from domain-specific activation geometry, SafeDIG enables position-aware sparse feature transfer. The method demonstrates improved robustness and generalization compared to fixed-layer safety steering approaches, successfully steering the model away from harmful outputs while preserving generation quality across both known and shifted target risk domains.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that safety in generative models requires understanding their internal representations and generation dynamics rather than relying solely on prompt filtering or output detection. It extends prior research on mechanistic safety interventions and sparse feature analysis by applying these techniques to the multi-scale, cross-modal nature of Diffusion Transformers. SafeDIG responds to challenges identified in recent work on diffusion model safety, where harmful semantics progressively entangle with visual rendering across layers. The approach combines insights from mechanistic interpretability, domain adaptation literature, and sparse autoencoder methods that have been increasingly applied to understand and steer neural networks. It also reflects broader efforts to move beyond brittle, dataset-specific safety measures toward more robust and generalizable control mechanisms.
enables: SafeDIG opens pathways for more reliable safety mechanisms in increasingly capable text-to-image generators by demonstrating that position-aware intervention can achieve both robustness and generalization. The sparse safety dictionary approach suggests future work on building reusable, composable safety modules that transfer across model architectures and risk domains, potentially enabling safety researchers to focus interventions on critical bottleneck positions. The framework's ability to separate transferable from domain-specific features could inform safer design of multi-modal systems and motivate research into understanding which layers and positions are inherently more amenable to stable safety steering. This work may also catalyze investigation into automated discovery of optimal intervention sites and development of safety specifications that explicitly account for distributional shift in harmful content.
